name: Andromeda Galaxy
blurb: the nearest big spiral galaxy
seen: a faint elongated oval; binoculars and dark skies help

name: Orion Nebula
blurb: a glowing stellar nursery in Orion’s sword
seen: a soft grey-green misty patch around a knot of stars

name: Pleiades
blurb: the Seven Sisters — a sparkling young cluster
seen: a tight dipper-shaped knot of bright blue stars, naked-eye

name: Beehive Cluster
blurb: a swarm of stars in Cancer
seen: a faint fuzzy patch to the eye; a field of stars in binoculars

name: Hercules Cluster
blurb: the finest globular in the northern sky
seen: a round fuzzy ball; grainy with a telescope

name: Ring Nebula
blurb: a dying star’s smoke ring
seen: a tiny grey smoke-ring — a telescope helps

name: Dumbbell Nebula
blurb: a bright planetary nebula in Vulpecula
seen: a soft glowing apple-core shape in binoculars

name: Whirlpool Galaxy
blurb: a face-on spiral with a companion
seen: a faint round glow; spiral hints need a big scope

name: Bode's Galaxy
blurb: a bright spiral in Ursa Major
seen: an oval glow, bright core; binoculars show it

name: Sombrero Galaxy
blurb: an edge-on galaxy with a dark dust lane
seen: a small elongated glow; the lane needs a scope

name: Lagoon Nebula
blurb: a big bright nebula in Sagittarius
seen: a misty glow with an embedded cluster, naked-eye in dark skies

name: Trifid Nebula
blurb: a nebula split by dark dust lanes
seen: a faint patch; the trifurcation needs a scope

name: Eagle Nebula
blurb: home of the Pillars of Creation
seen: a cluster wrapped in faint nebulosity

name: Omega Nebula
blurb: a bright swan-shaped nebula
seen: a glowing checkmark/swan shape in binoculars

name: Sagittarius Cluster
blurb: one of the brightest globulars
seen: a bright round fuzzball low in the south

name: M4
blurb: a nearby loose globular near Antares
seen: a dim round glow beside a bright red star

name: M3
blurb: a rich spring globular
seen: a compact fuzzy ball

name: M15
blurb: a dense globular in Pegasus
seen: a tight bright fuzzball

name: Wild Duck Cluster
blurb: a rich, compact open cluster
seen: a dense fan of faint stars in binoculars

name: Ptolemy Cluster
blurb: a big bright cluster in the Scorpion’s tail
seen: a sparkling naked-eye scatter of stars

name: Butterfly Cluster
blurb: a butterfly-shaped cluster
seen: a small spray of stars near M7

name: Pinwheel Galaxy
blurb: a big face-on spiral
seen: a large, very faint round glow — dark skies essential

name: Triangulum Galaxy
blurb: a nearby face-on spiral
seen: a big, very faint glow; easier in binoculars than a scope

name: Owl Nebula
blurb: a planetary nebula with two dark "eyes"
seen: a round grey disc — a scope is needed

name: Black Eye Galaxy
blurb: a galaxy with a dark dust "black eye"
seen: a small oval glow; the dark lane needs aperture

name: Crab Nebula
blurb: the wreck of a supernova seen in 1054
seen: a faint grey oval smudge

name: M92
blurb: a fine globular overshadowed by M13
seen: a compact round fuzzball

name: Sunflower Galaxy
blurb: a flocculent spiral in Canes Venatici
seen: an elongated faint glow

name: Double Cluster
blurb: a stunning pair of clusters in Perseus
seen: two side-by-side knots of stars, naked-eye in dark skies

name: Sculptor Galaxy
blurb: a bright edge-on galaxy
seen: a long faint streak low in the south
